items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.q: How does a securely attached infant respond to reunion with the caregiver?
items.correct: Seeks comfort and is easily soothed, then returns to play.
items.misconception: Shows no distress and ignores the caregiver.
items.random_wrong: Cries continuously and cannot be comforted.
items.why: Students confuse secure with avoidant. Secure infants DO show distress at separation and seek comfort at reunion - this contact-seeking is the marker of security, not indifference.

items.q: According to Maslow, when can self-actualisation be achieved?
items.correct: Only after lower deficiency needs are largely met.
items.misconception: Anyone can self-actualise at any time through willpower.
items.why: Students miss the hierarchical structure. You cannot pursue growth needs while physiological, safety, love, and esteem needs are unmet - that's the whole point of a hierarchy.

items.q: The Strange Situation classification 'Type A' is:
items.why: It's Insecure-avoidant. “Secure” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Bowlby's 44 Thieves study supported his concept of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Maternal deprivation. “Monotropy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many studies did Van IJzendoorn's 1988 meta-analysis include?
items.why: Exact answer: 32 studies. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
